text:
SIMD offers an alternative to the perf hit of not aligning with LLVM — if your new language primitive can be expressed efficiently in terms of SIMD, then missing out on the LLVM optimizations doesn't matter to you.

This is of course just a theory. For one, I don't have any particular primitive in mind. For two, I don't know enough about LLVM — perhaps there is, in fact, no computation that can be accelerated by SIMD that wouldn't be meaningfully slowed for lack of compiler optimizations.

text: Also I'll just point out that I'm getting a vibe from a lot of people that their "future of coding" involves C-like performance. Most applications aren't limited by operations per second... so I'm puzzled by why people are limiting their visions to ones with a clear mapping to C-like constructs. Only asymptotic complexity matters broadly, since that is what separates the possible from the impossible.

text:
LLVM and its gigantic array of optimizations, is insanely complex, with diminishing benefits for ordinary programs. I once did a test of a huge program and compiled it with lots of optimizations, and then compiled down to 486 era only instructions. About 1% difference. The sad truth is that CPU's are so fast now that both sides of a branch are executed, and many things that used to cost don't; but what determines your overall performance is how you have your data arranged in memory. If you can keep from hopping around RAM and stay in the caches your program runs like the wind. That's why some of the C programs are so fast, is that they were laid out very carefully from a data structure point of view, and why Java is notorious slow, because it sprays objects all over the heap. 99% of computers are idle nowadays; outside of big data and very specialized applications performance is really the last place to put your effort. Ease of maintenance trumps CPU costs in the vast majority of applications. That being said, there is no excuse for the sweathogs that we see today, where hundreds of megabytes are used to draw a single tab  window in Chrome, etc.

It is also true that CPU's are often advertised as being much faster than they really are. I once did a benchmark on a fairly CPU intensive app, and found that an e3 was faster than a far more expensive Xeon processor with many cores, because this was a mostly single threaded program, and adding all the cores trades off performance for single threaded. Also i discovered that some programs are faster with hyperthreading turned off, particularly those that do lots of interrupts because pretending you have more cores than you really do entails tradeoffs as well. Intel's product line is bewildering and a lot of products are sold for huge premiums just because they have some super rare instructions that spy agencies want. There's lots of nonsense out there. Intel hasn't added a useful instruction since they added true random numbers; most of the new ones are crazy; designed primarily to waste the engineering time of the cloners in China.

text: Reminds me of a Ruby interpreter story from a while back.  (I forget the details.)  But the gist is that the struct for a Ruby object is generally five words in size.  *Padding* the size to eight words yielded a performance improvement from lining up better CPU caches.

text: Alignment used to matter quite a bit. I don't think so any more, because so much pipelining is happening. They have so many billions of transistors to throw around, but RAM is holding the whole thing back. RAM speeds have only slightly increased compared to CPU power; billions awaits to the company that creates a breakthrough. DRAM has hardly changed. And is the main cost in servers. A few years ago, Samsung had a terrible recall for the Note cellphones that caught on fire; they lost billions and made it up by tripling RAM prices, and then everyone else raised their prices. Took years to come back down again. There is a revolution coming where the RAM will no longer be volatile. That is a very interesting change that i have planned for in my Beads system. Writing to the disk will be a thing of the past.

text:
> Also I'll just point out that I'm getting a vibe from a lot of people that their "future of coding" involves C-like performance. Most applications aren't limited by operations per second... so I'm puzzled by why people are limiting their visions to ones with a clear mapping to C-like constructs. Only asymptotic complexity matters broadly, since that is what separates the possible from the impossible.
I didn't mean to suggest that C like performance is necessary. In fact I think optimising compilers are very bad for understanding. The examples I gave were cases of asymptotic complexity being different, and in high level languages. Shalabh's async track trace example is one that has nothing to do with performance.

For me it's not about the performance so much as the tension of presenting a model of computation that is fundamentally at odds with reality. The disagreement is whether or not this matters. The answer to this question basically comes down to how airtight your abstractions are. My main point is these issues can be avoided if you just make it possible to draw back the curtain.

If your abstraction is blocking my understanding, let me easily remove it. If your abstraction is *not* blocking my understanding, whether or not I can remove it is moot because I won't want to.

text: If you want to draw such shapes at good quality, I can recommend the nanovg library; you just need an opengl render target and can draw high quality images.

elements.text: If you want to draw such shapes at good quality, I can recommend the nanovg library; you just need an opengl render target and can draw high quality images.

text: If a language has a bad abstraction, then the solution is to replace it with a better abstraction, not expose the machine code (the "reality") that the bad abstraction compiles to. I can't think of any situation in which exposing the machine code is a good idea, because if the user has to think about machine code, then my language has failed to achieve its purpose, which is to be a new foundation for specifying & reasoning about computation. What I *do* want to expose via my programming environment is the time & space complexity of programs, so the user can reason about what is possible and impossible. If the language is designed well, then the environment can provide an intuitive explanation for why a program has a particular complexity.

text: The CS notions of time and space complexity have never been based on CPU instructions or memory bits. They talk about the rate of growth of time and space measured in abstract "operations" and "stored data" relative to the "input size" of your program, the concrete units being irrelevant (since we're interested in the slope).

text: You can't provide an exact number for operations or data storage regardless of what units you use, until code actually runs. The number of concrete operations in any program with a branch instruction varies with the input, but its (worst case) time complexity will be fixed, across all inputs.

text:
Yes, but specifically in your language, how are you measuring "operations"? Surely in a way that maps very closely to x86 instructions? Otherwise, what's the value of this information?

Obviously it's possible to define some notion of "operation" that treats add(a, b), sqrt(n) and factorize(n) all as "one operation", and then discuss time complexity in terms of these - but this is not an analysis that is particularly useful.

We have actually built machines that map "memory space" to metres (RAM), and "instruction count" to seconds (CPUs), which is why time and space complexity analysis (in these terms) is useful to humans.

text: I’ll ask you the same question: how do you measure operations in a C program? An x86 program? How useful is that measure? Whatever measure I choose would be no worse.

text: For that reason, I’m not going to tell users how long a program did/might run by operation count, like “173”. It’s not useful. I could tell them the runtime in seconds, if they need to benchmark.

text: (Spoiler: you can’t specify how long an instruction is going to take across all CPUs, not even all x86 CPUs, nor all Intel x86 CPUs. It varies by model. You also can’t predict how long any memory access is going to take in any multi-threaded or multi-process system, which is all consumer systems).

text: (Spoiler: you can’t specify how long an instruction is going to take across all CPUs, not even all x86 CPUs, nor all Intel x86 CPUs. It varies by model. You also can’t predict how long any memory access is going to take in any multi-threaded or multi-process system, which is all consumer systems).

text:
Hi!
Today we launched Fibery. It is a connected workspace for teams. In a nutshell, you can build your internal tools in Fibery with no-code. It would be great to have a feedback of this super wise community :slightly_smiling_face: <https://www.producthunt.com/posts/fibery-3>

attachments.title: Fibery - Connected workspace for teams | Product Hunt
attachments.text: Create a workspace that brings teams together: sales :hearts: marketing :hearts: product :hearts: R&D. Escape costly scattered tools and unreliable integrations by having goals, projects, customers, and docs connected in Fibery.
attachments.fallback: Product Hunt: Fibery - Connected workspace for teams | Product Hunt

If you mean high level vision, here is it <https://medium.com/fibery/fibery-getting-started-64bd0839c85a>

If you mean technologies behind, then we don’t have it so far. In short it was built on Clojure (core for flexible domain) + Javascript for quite many side services + Postgres (works surprisingly well for flexible domain, we selected it instead of graph database).

Intentionally, without any context, I want to share a concept to see what peoples temperature of it is. `Dialog Driven Development`
What does that mean to you? Does it seem inviting/interesting/futuristic/inclusive?

text: Now, for more context. Think Star Trek when Pike talks to the computer. He can ask complex questions and create complex procedures with just his voice, but the computer will have a dialog with him to get all the necessary context to achieve what he is asking for. As the idea of Dialog Driven Development is to focus more on the conversation with the user instead diagram/visual development and traditional coding.

text: I think prototype conversations would be really interesting to see. Like, given the user is trying to write a calculator, what is the ideal back and forth that lets them do that. My hypothesis is that these would demonstrate that the software needs to already have sophisticated abstractions that make a lot of decisions for you. I think without that, the decision tree would get too deep/complex.

text: <@UKDM3GLAJ> Thank you for your insight :slightly_smiling_face: I think I’m getting a different kind of question from you: What is the domain? I would not expect someone to build a calculator with voice but I would expect them to, for example, track high-value customers with open support tickets. More EUSE cases than GPPL cases.

text: <@UMYKGHYBA> Cool stuff! I reviewed your videos and content. Great messaging too! Your videos are hilarious, great work, they feel really inclusive. I got the impression this was an alternative to perhaps Notion, Airtable, Asana, Jira or Basecamp. Focused mostly on CRM and knowledge. The disconnect for me was the no-code aspects, the concepts of “installing” templates felt strange (why not just “use” or “add”?) and the general positioning of the product feels at the crossroads of many tools that already exist in the market today. I empathize with the “all-in-one” approach however, the more and more you get all-in-one the more opinions start to form and the more rigid the product gets. I’m currently using Asana (IMO Jira is far to cumbersome) and Asana is really user freindly with a crazy amount of features,  I tried to keep an open mind yet at the same time I was thinking what really differentiates Fibrery to Asana? In my mind, this all circles back to it’s a project management tool. I would love to hear your reaction to this and see if you can help me fill in the gaps. Overall, I’m impressed and interested. I’m mostly curious as to what the largest customer/use case you have.

text:
Very first, high level impression/feelings I get when I hear the term:
- I think smth like "cool, sounds like I will be guided, which means it could be easy/simple to develop smth"
- on the other hand, it reminds of experience with Google assistant or similar solutions, which means it will be relatively slow and while I will be impressed at start, I will quickly get stuck with it not understanding me.

It would certainly interest me to learn more about it though, I would click on "Learn more" or "Try out".

text: A coroutine is a lightweight cooperative thread. An example use might be waiting on asynchronous operations (like i/o) without loosing the state you’ve built up in your call stack - as you’d end up having to construct an even more complex (and likely inscrutable) state machine otherwise. So when you make your call to wait on an i/o operation, behind the scenes it sets aside your current stack and continues some other coroutine (or returns to the event loop). When the i/o operation event occurs, that callback resumes (switches to) the previously paused coroutine’s stack. In this way, you can writing asynchronous code as if it were synchronous, while still being aware of where those points are via things like naming conventions on methods that may pause the coroutine. You can also yield coroutines at safe points so they can do long running computations with stacks.

text:
I was chatting about the apparent lack of a dialog interface in "big data" type offerings. I'm not at all up to speed in the area; so there may be existing offerings I'm not aware of. The discussion was with a non-tech user who has to ask for custom reports to be built by his technical colleague; and would rather ask his platform- "Show me clients with highest percent increase in revenue in last two weeks of March"
Rather specific; but its on my list of things to look into; as it seems like a reasonable fit

text:
I like “dialog” because it implies both natural language and interaction/feedback.

One idle thought — is there a better word than “development”? That’s still kind of programmer jargon. For most people it probably means something different.

text:
Hi! I’m a newcomer from Almaty, Kazakhstan. Thanks for the invite <@UFPPABQ7P>! I moved to the US 8 years ago to go to boarding school. After getting kicked out & repeating junior year 3 times, I ended up studying entrepreneurship at New York University. There, I launched & failed my first startup, built the biggest entrepreneurship community & worked at a few accelerators. I also became obsessed with discovering and trying new tools and products.

After I graduated last month, I moved to Palo Alto with my mom (55), who is starting a new business and restarted studies at Stanford. So pumped for her!

I’m working on building a remote school that teaches people how to be more efficient at work using nocode and visual programming tools. I think this could be something like Lambda School but I’m doing more research on that. I’m running two experiments until figure out the model:

1. <http://optemization.com/home|Optemization>: agency that helps startups build & automate ops without code. Made $16,520.
2. <http://optemization.com/automagic-upcoming|Automagic>: production & curation of no-code & ops content. Got 234 subscribers (pre-launch).
</lifestory>

text: :wave: really cool <@U01176YGU4W>! I’m interested in knowing more about your work in progress with the remote school, I worked in different coding bootcamps and I feel that learning to code in such bootcamps sucks for people that only want to create their own products :joy:
